validation.comparison_result: Q3 results match ground truth: 10 rows expected, 10 rows actual

Q3.notes: Correctness verified. Performance: 380ms vs DuckDB target 77-124ms (3.4x slower than best). Zone maps reduce I/O by ~50%. SIMD and parallel execution active.

Q3.bottleneck: I/O-bound on HDD + join strategy inefficiency + suboptimal hash table for aggregation
Q3.root_cause: Despite zone map pruning reducing scans by ~50%, still reading 39.7M rows from HDD (7.3M orders + 32.4M lineitem = ~1.2GB data). Bitmap-based join arrays waste memory (60MB for orders_bitmap with max_orderkey=60M). Using std::unordered_map for 114K aggregation groups is 2-5x slower than compact hash tables. No Bloom filter to prune lineitem probes early.

analysis.overall: Query Q3 is correct and performs reasonably (380ms), but is 3.4x slower than DuckDB's best time (77ms). The query is I/O-bound on HDD with large scans despite zone maps. Key opportunities: (1) Bloom filter on filtered orders to reduce lineitem probe work by 70-80%, (2) Replace std::unordered_map with Swiss Tables or Robin Hood hashing for 2-5x faster aggregation, (3) Use compact hash table instead of bitmap arrays for joins to reduce memory footprint and improve cache locality, (4) Consider column prefetching to overlap I/O with computation on HDD.

performance_optimizations.technique: Bloom filter on filtered orders to prune lineitem probes
performance_optimizations.description: After filtering orders (1.46M qualifying), build a Bloom filter on the filtered o_orderkey values. During lineitem scan, check Bloom filter BEFORE probing orders_bitmap. This will eliminate ~70-80% of unnecessary bitmap lookups for non-matching lineitem rows. Current implementation checks all 32.4M lineitem rows against orders_bitmap; with Bloom filter, we only probe bitmap for ~6-10M rows that pass the filter. Bloom filter size: 1.46M keys * 4 bits = ~730KB (fits in L3). Expected false positive rate: 10% with 4 bits/element.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% reduction in lineitem processing time by skipping ~25M bitmap probes. Estimated speedup: 380ms → 300-320ms (net gain: 60-80ms). Reasoning: Bitmap probe is cheap but not free (~1-2ns/lookup = 25M * 2ns = 50ms overhead). Bloom filter adds ~1ns overhead per check but saves bitmap lookup for 80% of rows.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::unordered_map with compact hash table (Swiss Tables / Robin Hood hashing)
performance_optimizations.description: Replace std::unordered_map<AggKey, AggValue> in aggregation (lines 662, 757) with a compact hash table implementation. Swiss Tables (Google's abseil::flat_hash_map) or Robin Hood hashing provide 2-5x faster lookups and inserts compared to std::unordered_map due to better cache locality (open addressing vs chaining). For Q3's 114K aggregation groups, Swiss Tables will reduce aggregation overhead significantly. Implementation: Include abseil library and replace std::unordered_map with absl::flat_hash_map. Pre-size to 150K as already done (line 759). Alternative: Implement simple Robin Hood hashing inline without external dependency.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 30-50% reduction in aggregation time. Aggregation is ~20-30% of total query time (estimated 80-120ms), so speedup = 80-120ms * 0.4 = 32-48ms net gain. Estimated total: 380ms → 332-348ms. Reasoning: std::unordered_map has poor cache locality due to chaining (pointer chasing). Swiss Tables use SIMD for parallel slot comparison and open addressing for contiguous storage.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace bitmap arrays with compact hash tables for join state
performance_optimizations.description: Replace orders_bitmap/orders_orderdate/orders_shippriority arrays (lines 536-538) with a single compact hash table: absl::flat_hash_map<int32_t, OrderInfo> where OrderInfo = {orderdate, shippriority}. Current implementation allocates max_orderkey+1 elements (~60M entries, 60MB for bitmap + 480MB for data arrays = 540MB total) even though only 1.46M orders qualify (~2% density). Compact hash table will use ~50MB for 1.46M entries (35 bytes/entry including overhead), saving 490MB and improving cache locality during lineitem probe phase. Similar optimization for customer_bitmap: replace with absl::flat_hash_set<int32_t> (300K customers, ~10MB vs 60MB bitmap).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15% speedup from better cache locality during lineitem probes. Memory footprint reduction from 600MB to ~60MB improves cache hit rate. Estimated: 380ms → 340-355ms (net gain: 25-40ms). Reasoning: Bitmap arrays have poor spatial locality when keys are sparse (2% density). Hash table clusters active entries together. On 64-core system with 44MB L3, keeping join state in L3 is critical.

performance_optimizations.technique: Asynchronous I/O prefetching for lineitem columns
performance_optimizations.description: On HDD, I/O is the primary bottleneck (scanning 1.2GB of data). Current implementation uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint but processes serially (load column → scan → next column). Use asynchronous prefetching: after loading l_orderkey and starting scan, immediately issue MADV_WILLNEED for l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_shipdate to trigger background readahead. Alternatively, use mmap with MAP_POPULATE for hot columns to force immediate page-in. This overlaps I/O latency with computation. On HDD with 100MB/s sequential throughput, loading 800MB of lineitem columns takes ~8 seconds; overlapping reduces wall-clock time.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% reduction in I/O wait time by overlapping column loads with computation. Estimated: 380ms → 270-305ms (net gain: 75-110ms). Reasoning: Current I/O is sequential but serial. HDD can sustain ~100MB/s reads. Prefetching next column while processing current column hides ~2-3 seconds of I/O latency per column. However, with 64 threads, actual I/O is already somewhat overlapped. Conservative estimate: 20-25% speedup.

performance_optimizations.technique: Fuse customer filtering + bitmap building into single vectorized loop
performance_optimizations.description: Current customer filter (lines 461-479) scans c_mktsegment and builds customer_bitmap in separate passes per morsel. Fuse this into a single tight loop and vectorize the equality check using SIMD. Use AVX2 to compare 32 bytes (32 mktsegment codes) at once against building_code. This reduces cache misses and exploits instruction-level parallelism. Customer scan is small (1.5M rows) but improving it reduces query startup latency. Implementation: Add SIMD loop similar to date filtering (lines 558-578).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10ms reduction in customer filtering time (currently ~20-30ms). Minor but easy win. Estimated: 380ms → 370-375ms. Reasoning: Customer table is small (1.5M rows, ~6MB for c_mktsegment column). SIMD can process 32 comparisons per instruction (AVX2 with 8-bit codes). Speedup factor: ~8-16x for tight loop, but overall impact is limited by Amdahl's law (customer filter is <10% of total time).

performance_optimizations.technique: Use top-K heap incremental maintenance during aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation aggregates all 114K groups, then extracts top-10 (lines 774-798). Instead, maintain a min-heap of size 10 during the aggregation phase. Each thread maintains a local top-10 heap, then merge thread-local heaps at the end. This reduces final merge overhead from O(114K) to O(64 * 10 * log(10)) = O(640) operations. Memory footprint also drops from storing 114K groups to storing ~10K groups (one heap per thread) + top-10 final. However, this optimization is minor since sorting/merging 114K entries is fast (<5ms).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5ms reduction in final sorting/top-K extraction. Minimal impact. Estimated: 380ms → 375-378ms. Reasoning: Current priority_queue operations on 114K items take ~5-10ms. Incremental heaps reduce this to <1ms. However, 114K is small enough that this is not a bottleneck. Worth implementing only if aggregation cardinality were 10M+.

summary: Q3 iteration 1 is CORRECT and executes in 380ms, but is 3.4x slower than DuckDB's best (77ms). The code is already well-optimized with zone maps, SIMD, bitmaps, and 64-thread parallelism. Main bottlenecks: (1) I/O-bound on HDD despite zone maps (~1.2GB scanned), (2) Suboptimal join strategy using sparse bitmap arrays (2% density, 600MB footprint), (3) std::unordered_map for aggregation (2-5x slower than Swiss Tables). Top recommendations: Add Bloom filter on filtered orders to skip 70-80% of lineitem probes (Priority 1), replace std::unordered_map with compact hash tables (Priority 2), replace bitmap arrays with hash tables for better cache locality (Priority 3). Expected combined speedup: 380ms → 220-270ms (target: 77-124ms, would close gap to 2-3x vs DuckDB).
